(Pedro de Alcântara João Carlos Leopoldo Salvador Bibiano Francisco Xavier de Paula Leocádio Miguel Gabriel Rafael Gonzaga; 2 December 1825 – 5 December 1891), known as **"the Magnanimous"** ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *O Magnânimo*),[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman199985-1) was the [second and final emperor](/wiki/List_of_monarchs_of_Brazil "List of monarchs of Brazil") of the [Empire of Brazil](/wiki/Empire_of_Brazil "Empire of Brazil"). He reigned from 1831 until his deposition in the [military coup of 1889](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_Republic_(Brazil) "Proclamation of the Republic (Brazil)"), presiding over the longest and most stable reign in Brazilian history.[[a]](#cite_note-2)

*[Dom](/wiki/Don_(honorific) "Don (honorific)")* **Pedro II** (Pedro de Alcântara João Carlos Leopoldo Salvador Bibiano Francisco Xavier de Paula Leocádio Miguel Gabriel Rafael Gonzaga; 2 December 1825 – 5 December 1891), known as **"the Magnanimous"** ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *O Magnânimo*),[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman199985-1) was the [second and final emperor](/wiki/List_of_monarchs_of_Brazil "List of monarchs of Brazil") of the [Empire of Brazil](/wiki/Empire_of_Brazil "Empire of Brazil"). He reigned from 1831 until his deposition in the [military coup of 1889](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_Republic_(Brazil) "Proclamation of the Republic (Brazil)"), presiding over the longest and most stable reign in Brazilian history.[[a]](#cite_note-2)

Born in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), Pedro II was the seventh child of [Emperor Pedro I](/wiki/Pedro_I_of_Brazil "Pedro I of Brazil") and [Empress Maria Leopoldina](/wiki/Maria_Leopoldina_of_Austria "Maria Leopoldina of Austria"). [His father's abdication](/wiki/Abdication_of_Pedro_I_of_Brazil "Abdication of Pedro I of Brazil") and departure for Europe in 1831 left the five-year-old prince as emperor, ushering in a regency period marked by political instability and shaping a childhood dominated by rigorous education and preparation for rule. These formative years profoundly influenced his character, instilling a strong sense of duty, intellectual curiosity, and devotion to public service, alongside a growing personal ambivalence toward monarchy.

During his long reign, Pedro II transformed Brazil from a fragile post-colonial state into a consolidated and internationally respected power. His government was characterized by political stability, [freedom of speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_speech "Freedom of speech"), respect for [civil rights](/wiki/Civil_rights "Civil rights"), economic growth, and the functioning of a [constitutional parliamentary system](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"). Brazil achieved military success in conflicts such as the [Platine War](/wiki/Platine_War "Platine War"), the [Uruguayan War](/wiki/Uruguayan_War "Uruguayan War"), and the [Paraguayan War](/wiki/Paraguayan_War "Paraguayan War"), while also resolving numerous internal revolts and diplomatic disputes. Despite resistance from powerful interests, Pedro II ultimately supported and enabled the [abolition of slavery in Brazil](/wiki/Abolition_of_slavery_in_Brazil "Abolition of slavery in Brazil"), a defining achievement of his reign.

A patron of education, culture, and science, Pedro II gained international recognition as a learned and enlightened ruler. Nevertheless, in 1889 he was [overthrown in a coup d'état](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_Republic_(Brazil) "Proclamation of the Republic (Brazil)") with limited popular backing, led primarily by military elites favoring a republican regime. Disillusioned and unwilling to provoke civil conflict, he accepted exile without resistance. Pedro II spent his final years in Europe, living modestly and largely alone.

Though deposed at the height of his personal popularity, the monarchy itself was actively delegitimized by the succeeding republican government. In the decades that followed, as Brazil's republics proved turbulent and often authoritarian, historians reassessed the imperial era more favorably — repatriating his remains to Brazil with national honors and consistently ranking him among the greatest statesmen in Brazilian history, with many considering him the greatest Brazilian ruler.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Main article: [Early life of Pedro II of Brazil](/wiki/Early_life_of_Pedro_II_of_Brazil "Early life of Pedro II of Brazil")

### Birth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Birth")]

[![Framed oval head and shoulders portrait of an infant boy](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Pedro_II_1826_frame_removed.png/250px-Pedro_II_1826_frame_removed.png)](/wiki/File:Pedro_II_1826_frame_removed.png)

Pedro at 10 months old, 1826

Pedro was born at 02:30 on 2 December 1825 in the [Palace of São Cristóvão](/wiki/Palace_of_S%C3%A3o_Crist%C3%B3v%C3%A3o "Palace of São Cristóvão"), in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), Brazil.[[2]](#cite_note-3) Named after [St. Peter of Alcantara](/wiki/St._Peter_of_Alcantara "St. Peter of Alcantara"), his name in full was Pedro de Alcântara João Carlos Leopoldo Salvador Bibiano Francisco Xavier de Paula Leocádio Miguel Gabriel Rafael Gonzaga.[[3]](#cite_note-4) Through his father, Emperor [Dom](/wiki/Dom_(title) "Dom (title)") [Pedro I](/wiki/Pedro_I_of_Brazil "Pedro I of Brazil"), he was a member of the Brazilian branch of the [House of Braganza](/wiki/House_of_Braganza "House of Braganza") ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Bragança*) and was referred to using the honorific *Dom* ([Lord](/wiki/Lord "Lord")) from birth.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999424-5) He was the grandson of King Dom [João VI of Portugal](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_VI_of_Portugal "João VI of Portugal") and nephew of Dom [Miguel I](/wiki/Miguel_I "Miguel I").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBesouchet199340-6)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz199847-7) His mother was the Archduchess [Maria Leopoldina of Austria](/wiki/Maria_Leopoldina_of_Austria "Maria Leopoldina of Austria"), daughter of [Franz II](/wiki/Francis_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Francis II, Holy Roman Emperor"), the last [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor"). Through his mother, Pedro was a nephew of [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte") and first cousin of Emperors [Napoleon II](/wiki/Napoleon_II "Napoleon II") of [France](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire"), [Franz Joseph I](/wiki/Franz_Joseph_I_of_Austria "Franz Joseph I of Austria") of [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") and [Don](/wiki/Don_(honorific) "Don (honorific)") [Maximiliano I](/wiki/Maximilian_I_of_Mexico "Maximilian I of Mexico") of [Mexico](/wiki/Second_Mexican_Empire "Second Mexican Empire").[[7]](#cite_note-8)

The only legitimate male child of Pedro I to survive infancy, he was officially recognized as heir apparent to the Brazilian throne with the title Prince Imperial on 6 August 1826.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002198-9)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon19755-10) Empress Maria Leopoldina died on 11 December 1826, a few days after a stillbirth, when Pedro was a year old.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon197515-11)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBesouchet199341-12) Two and a half years later, his father married Princess [Amélie of Leuchtenberg](/wiki/Am%C3%A9lie_of_Leuchtenberg "Amélie of Leuchtenberg"). Prince Pedro developed an affectionate relationship with her, whom he came to regard as his mother.[[12]](#cite_note-13) Pedro I's desire to restore his daughter [Maria II](/wiki/Maria_II_of_Portugal "Maria II of Portugal") to her Portuguese throne, which had been usurped by his brother Miguel I, as well as his declining political position at home led to his abrupt abdication on 7 April 1831.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho200721-14)[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_115-15) He and Amélie immediately departed for Europe, leaving behind the Prince Imperial, who became Emperor Dom Pedro II.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlivieri19995-16)[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman199929-17)

### Early coronation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early coronation")]

[![Three-quarters length painted portrait of the pre-adolescent Pedro in gold-embroidered tunic with a sash of office and hat tucked under his right arm and left hand resting on the pommel of his sword](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/RetratodompedroIIcrianca.JPG/250px-RetratodompedroIIcrianca.JPG)](/wiki/File:RetratodompedroIIcrianca.JPG)

Pedro II at age 12 wearing court dress and the [Order of the Golden Fleece](/wiki/Order_of_the_Golden_Fleece "Order of the Golden Fleece"), 1838

Upon leaving the country, Emperor Pedro I selected three people to take charge of his son and remaining daughters. The first was [José Bonifácio de Andrada](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Bonif%C3%A1cio_de_Andrada "José Bonifácio de Andrada"), his friend and an influential leader during [Brazilian independence](/wiki/Brazilian_independence "Brazilian independence"), who was named guardian.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_117-18)[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz199850-19) The second was [Mariana de Verna](/wiki/Mariana_Carlota_de_Verna_Magalh%C3%A3es_Coutinho,_Countess_of_Belmonte "Mariana Carlota de Verna Magalhães Coutinho, Countess of Belmonte"), who had held the post of *aia* ([governess](/wiki/Governess "Governess")) since the birth of Pedro II.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho200731-20) As a child, the then-Prince Imperial called her "*Dadama*", as he could not pronounce the word *dama* ([Lady](/wiki/Lady "Lady")) correctly.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon19755-10) He regarded her as his surrogate mother and would continue to call her by her nickname well into adulthood out of affection.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman199929-17)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBesouchet199339-21) The third person was Rafael, an [Afro-Brazilian](/wiki/Afro-Brazilian "Afro-Brazilian") veteran of the [Cisplatine War](/wiki/Cisplatine_War "Cisplatine War").[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho200731-20)[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon197557-22) He was an employee in the Palace of São Cristóvão whom Pedro I deeply trusted and asked to look after his son—a charge that he carried out for the rest of his life.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002198-9)[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon197557-22)

Bonifácio was dismissed from his position in December 1832 and replaced by another guardian.[[22]](#cite_note-23) Pedro II spent his days studying, with only two hours set aside for amusements.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho200727-24)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlivieri19998-25) Though Pedro II was intelligent,[[25]](#cite_note-26) the hours of study were strenuous and the preparation for his role as monarch was demanding. He had few friends of his age and limited contact with his sisters. All that coupled with the sudden loss of his parents gave Pedro II an unhappy and lonely upbringing.[[26]](#cite_note-27) The environment in which he was raised turned him into a shy and needy person who saw books as a refuge and retreat from the real world.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho200729,_33-28)[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman199939-29)

The possibility of lowering the young Emperor's age of majority, instead of waiting until he turned 18, had been floated since 1835.[[29]](#cite_note-30) His elevation to the throne had led to a troublesome period of endless crises. The [regency](/wiki/Regency_period_(Empire_of_Brazil) "Regency period (Empire of Brazil)") created to [rule on his behalf](/wiki/Minority_reign "Minority reign") was plagued from the start by disputes between political factions and rebellions across the nation.[[30]](#cite_note-31) Those politicians who had risen to power during the 1830s had by now also become familiar with the pitfalls of rule. Historian Roderick J. Barman stated that by 1840, "they had lost all faith in their ability to rule the country on their own. They accepted Pedro II as an authority figure whose presence was indispensable for the country's survival".[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999317-32) When asked by politicians if he would like to assume full powers, Pedro II accepted.[[32]](#cite_note-33) On the following day, 23 July 1840, the [General Assembly](/wiki/General_Assembly_(Brazil) "General Assembly (Brazil)") (the Brazilian [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament "Parliament")) formally [declared the 14-year-old Pedro II of age](/wiki/Declaration_of_age_of_Pedro_II "Declaration of age of Pedro II").[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho200740-34) He was later [acclaimed, crowned, and consecrated](/wiki/Coronation_of_the_Emperor_of_Brazil "Coronation of the Emperor of Brazil") on 18 July 1841.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz199873-35)[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_172-36)

## Consolidation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Consolidation")]

Main article: [Consolidation of Pedro II of Brazil](/wiki/Consolidation_of_Pedro_II_of_Brazil "Consolidation of Pedro II of Brazil")

### Imperial authority established

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Imperial authority established")]

[![Full-length painted portrait of a blond young man standing in a garden dressed in white trousers, a military tunic with heavy gold braid, a blue sash of office, and holding a bicorn admiral's hat](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Pedro_II_of_Brazil_by_Rugendas_1846_original.jpg/250px-Pedro_II_of_Brazil_by_Rugendas_1846_original.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pedro_II_of_Brazil_by_Rugendas_1846_original.jpg)

Pedro II at age 20 wearing court dress, 1846

Removal of the factious regency brought stability to the government. Pedro II was seen nationwide as a legitimate source of authority, whose position placed him above partisanship and petty disputes. He was, however, still young as well as shy and insecure.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman199974–75-37) Behind the scenes, a group of high-ranking palace servants and notable politicians led by [Aureliano Coutinho](/wiki/Aureliano_Coutinho,_Viscount_of_Sepetiba "Aureliano Coutinho, Viscount of Sepetiba") (later Viscount of Sepetiba) became known as the "Courtier Faction" as they established influence over the young Emperor. Some were very close to him, such as Mariana de Verna and Steward Paulo Barbosa da Silva.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman199949-38) Pedro II was deftly used by the Courtiers against their actual or suspected foes.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman199980-39)

The Brazilian government secured the hand of Princess [Teresa Cristina](/wiki/Teresa_Cristina_of_the_Two_Sicilies "Teresa Cristina of the Two Sicilies") of the [Kingdom of the Two Sicilies](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Two_Sicilies "Kingdom of the Two Sicilies"). She and Pedro II were [married by proxy](/wiki/Married_by_proxy "Married by proxy") in [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples") on 30 May 1843.[[39]](#cite_note-40) Upon seeing her in person, the Emperor was noticeably disappointed.[[40]](#cite_note-41) Teresa Cristina was short, a bit overweight, and not considered conventionally pretty.[[41]](#cite_note-42) He did little to hide his disillusionment. One observer stated that he turned his back to Teresa Cristina, another depicted him as being so shocked that he needed to sit.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman199997-43) That evening, Pedro II wept and complained to Mariana de Verna, "They have deceived me, *Dadama*!"[[43]](#cite_note-barman97-44) It took several hours to convince him that duty demanded that he proceed.[[43]](#cite_note-barman97-44) The Nuptial Mass, with the ratification of the vows previously taken by proxy and the conferral of the nuptial blessing, occurred on the following day, 4 September.[[44]](#cite_note-45)

In late 1845 and early 1846, the Emperor made a tour of Brazil's southern provinces, traveling through [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo") (of which [Paraná](/wiki/Paran%C3%A1_(state) "Paraná (state)") was a part at this time), [Santa Catarina](/wiki/Santa_Catarina_(state) "Santa Catarina (state)") and [Rio Grande do Sul](/wiki/Rio_Grande_do_Sul "Rio Grande do Sul"). He was buoyed by the warm and enthusiastic responses he received.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999111-46) By then Pedro II had matured physically and mentally. He grew into a man who, at 1.90 meters (6 ft 3 in) tall with blue eyes and blond hair, was seen as handsome.[[46]](#cite_note-47) Over time, he developed greater confidence and became known for his diligence, impartiality, and courteous conduct. Barman said that he kept "his emotions under iron discipline. He was never rude and never lost his temper. He was exceptionally discreet in words and cautious in action."[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999109,_122-48) Most importantly, this period saw the end of the Courtier Faction. Pedro II began to fully exercise authority and successfully engineered the end of the courtiers' influence by removing them from his inner circle while avoiding any public disruption.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999109,_114-49)

### Abolition of the slave trade and war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Abolition of the slave trade and war")]

[![A head and shoulders daguerreotype portrait of a young man with short beard wearing a dark suit and cravat](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Pedro_II_of_Brazil_1848.jpg/250px-Pedro_II_of_Brazil_1848.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pedro_II_of_Brazil_1848.jpg)

Pedro II around age 22, c. 1848. This is the earliest surviving photograph of the Emperor

Pedro II was faced by three crises between 1848 and 1852.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999122-50) The first test came in confronting the trade in illegally imported slaves. This had been banned in 1826 as part of a treaty with the United Kingdom.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999123-51) Trafficking continued unabated, however, and the British government's passage of the [Slave Trade (Brazil) Act 1845](/wiki/Slave_Trade_(Brazil)_Act_1845 "Slave Trade (Brazil) Act 1845") ([8 & 9 Vict.](/wiki/8_%26_9_Vict. "8 & 9 Vict.") c. 122) authorized British warships to board Brazilian shipping and seize any found involved in the slave trade.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999122–123-52) While Brazil grappled with this problem, the [Praieira revolt](/wiki/Praieira_revolt "Praieira revolt") erupted on 6 November 1848. This was a conflict between local political factions within [Pernambuco](/wiki/Pernambuco "Pernambuco") province; it was suppressed by March 1849. The [Eusébio de Queirós Law](/wiki/Eus%C3%A9bio_de_Queir%C3%B3s_Law "Eusébio de Queirós Law") was [promulgated](/wiki/Promulgated "Promulgated") on 4 September 1850, which gave the Brazilian government broad authority to combat the illegal slave trade. With this new tool, Brazil moved to eliminate importation of slaves. By 1852, this first crisis was over, and Britain accepted that the trade had been suppressed.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999124-53)

The third crisis entailed a conflict with the [Argentine Confederation](/wiki/Argentine_Confederation "Argentine Confederation") regarding ascendancy over territories adjacent to the [Río de la Plata](/wiki/R%C3%ADo_de_la_Plata "Río de la Plata") and free navigation of that waterway.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999125-54) Since the 1830s, Argentine dictator [Juan Manuel de Rosas](/wiki/Juan_Manuel_de_Rosas "Juan Manuel de Rosas") had supported rebellions within [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay") and Brazil. It was only in 1850 that Brazil was able to address the threat posed by Rosas.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999125-54) An alliance was forged between Brazil, Uruguay and disaffected Argentines, leading to the [Platine War](/wiki/Platine_War "Platine War") and the subsequent overthrow of the Argentine ruler in February 1852.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999125–126-55)[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007102–103-56) Barman said that a "considerable portion of the credit must be ... assigned to the Emperor, whose cool head, tenacity of purpose, and sense of what was feasible proved indispensable."[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999122-50)

The Empire's successful navigation of these crises considerably enhanced the nation's stability and prestige, and Brazil emerged as a hemispheric power.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevine199963–64-57) Internationally, Europeans began to regard the country as embodying familiar liberal ideals, such as freedom of the press and constitutional respect for civil liberties. Its representative parliamentary monarchy also stood in stark contrast to the mix of dictatorships and instability endemic in the other nations of South America during this period.[[57]](#cite_note-58)

## Growth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Growth")]

Main article: [Growth of Pedro II of Brazil](/wiki/Growth_of_Pedro_II_of_Brazil "Growth of Pedro II of Brazil")

### Pedro II and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Pedro II and politics")]

[![Photographic half-length portrait of a seated bearded man dressed in a dark, double-breasted coat with his right hand tucked inside the front](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Pedro_II_of_Brazil_1851_edit.png/250px-Pedro_II_of_Brazil_1851_edit.png)](/wiki/File:Pedro_II_of_Brazil_1851_edit.png)

Pedro II around age 25, c.1851

At the beginning of the 1850s, Brazil enjoyed internal stability and economic prosperity.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999159-59)[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz1998100-60) Under the prime ministry of [Honório Hermeto Carneiro Leão](/wiki/Hon%C3%B3rio_Hermeto_Carneiro_Le%C3%A3o "Honório Hermeto Carneiro Leão") (then-Viscount and later Marquis of Paraná) the Emperor advanced his own ambitious program: the *conciliação* (conciliation) and *melhoramentos* (material developments).[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999162-61) Pedro II's reforms aimed to promote less political partisanship, and forward infrastructure and economic development. The nation was being interconnected through [railroad](/wiki/Railroad "Railroad"), [electrical telegraph](/wiki/Electrical_telegraph "Electrical telegraph"), and [steamship](/wiki/Steamship "Steamship") lines, uniting it into a single entity.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999159-59) The general opinion, both at home and abroad, was that these accomplishments had been possible due to Brazil's "governance as a [monarchy](/wiki/Monarchy "Monarchy") and the character of Pedro II".[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999159-59)

Pedro II was neither a British-style [figurehead](/wiki/Figurehead "Figurehead") nor an autocrat in the manner of [Russian czars](/wiki/Russian_czars "Russian czars"). The Emperor exercised power through cooperation with elected politicians, economic interests, and popular support.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999161–162-62) The active presence of Pedro II on the political scene was an important part of the government's structure, which also included the cabinet, the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_of_Brazil "Chamber of Deputies of Brazil") and the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Brazil "Senate of Brazil") (the latter two formed the General Assembly). He used his participation in directing the course of government as a means of influence. His direction became indispensable, although it never devolved into "one-man rule."[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999178-63) In his handling of the political parties, he "needed to maintain a reputation for impartiality, work in accord with the popular mood, and avoid any flagrant imposition of his will on the political scene."[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999120-64)

The Emperor's more notable political successes were achieved primarily because of the non-confrontational and cooperative manner with which he approached both issues and the partisan figures with whom he had to deal. He was remarkably tolerant, seldom taking offense at criticism, opposition or even incompetence.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999164-65) He did not have the constitutional authority to force acceptance of his initiatives without support, and his collaborative approach towards governing kept the nation progressing and enabled the political system to successfully function.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999165-66) The Emperor respected the prerogatives of the legislature, even when they resisted, delayed, or thwarted his goals and appointments.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999178–179-67) Most politicians appreciated and supported his role. Many had lived through the regency period, when the lack of an emperor who could stand above petty and special interests led to years of strife between political factions. Their experiences in public life had created a conviction that Pedro II was "indispensable to Brazil's continued peace and prosperity."[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999170-68)

### Domestic life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Domestic life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Fran%C3%A7ois-Ren%C3%A9_Moreaux_-_O_imperador_D._Pedro_II%2C_sua_esposa_Teresa_Cristina_e_suas_filhas%2C_princesas_Isabel_e_Leopoldina%2C_1857.JPG/250px-Fran%C3%A7ois-Ren%C3%A9_Moreaux_-_O_imperador_D._Pedro_II%2C_sua_esposa_Teresa_Cristina_e_suas_filhas%2C_princesas_Isabel_e_Leopoldina%2C_1857.JPG)](/wiki/File:Fran%C3%A7ois-Ren%C3%A9_Moreaux_-_O_imperador_D._Pedro_II,_sua_esposa_Teresa_Cristina_e_suas_filhas,_princesas_Isabel_e_Leopoldina,_1857.JPG)

Pedro II and [Teresa Cristina](/wiki/Teresa_Cristina_of_the_Two_Sicilies "Teresa Cristina of the Two Sicilies") with their surviving children Princesses [Leopoldina](/wiki/Princess_Leopoldina_of_Brazil "Princess Leopoldina of Brazil") and [Isabel](/wiki/Isabel,_Princess_Imperial_of_Brazil "Isabel, Princess Imperial of Brazil"), 1857

The marriage between Pedro II and Teresa Cristina started off badly. With maturity, patience and their first child, [Afonso](/wiki/Afonso,_Prince_Imperial_of_Brazil "Afonso, Prince Imperial of Brazil"), their relationship improved.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999126-69)[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho200773-70) Later Teresa Cristina gave birth to more children: [Isabel](/wiki/Isabel,_Princess_Imperial_of_Brazil "Isabel, Princess Imperial of Brazil"), in 1846; [Leopoldina](/wiki/Princess_Leopoldina_of_Brazil "Princess Leopoldina of Brazil"), in 1847; and lastly, [Pedro Afonso](/wiki/Pedro,_Prince_Imperial_of_Brazil "Pedro, Prince Imperial of Brazil"), in 1848.[[70]](#cite_note-71) Both boys died when very young, which devastated the Emperor and completely changed his view of the Empire's future.[[71]](#cite_note-72) Despite his affection for his daughters, he did not believe that Princess Isabel, although his heir, would have any chance of prospering on the throne. He felt his successor needed to be male for the monarchy to be viable.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999129–130-73) He increasingly saw the imperial system as being tied so inextricably to himself, that it would not survive him.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999130-74) Isabel and her sister received a remarkable education, although they were given no preparation for governing the nation. Pedro II excluded Isabel from participation in government business and decisions.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999151–152-75)

Sometime around 1850, Pedro II began having discreet affairs with other women.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999128-76) The most famous and enduring of these relationships involved [Luísa Margarida Portugal de Barros, Countess of Barral](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADsa_de_Barros,_Countess_of_Barral "Luísa de Barros, Countess of Barral"), with whom he formed a romantic and intimate, though not adulterous, friendship after she was appointed governess to the emperor's daughters in November 1856.[[76]](#cite_note-77) Throughout his life, the Emperor held onto a hope of finding a soulmate, something he felt cheated of due to the necessity of a [marriage of state](/wiki/Marriage_of_state "Marriage of state") to a woman for whom he never felt passion.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999144,_148-78) This is but one instance illustrating his dual identity: one who assiduously carried out his duty as emperor and another who considered the imperial office an unrewarding burden and who was happier in the worlds of literature and science.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho200780-79)

Pedro II maintained a demanding daily schedule. He typically woke at 7:00 and did not retire before 2:00 in the morning. Most of his day was spent on affairs of state, while his limited leisure time was devoted to reading and study.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999134-80) The Emperor went about his daily routine dressed in a simple black tail coat, trousers, and cravat. For special occasions he would wear court dress, and he only appeared in full regalia with [crown, mantle, and scepter](/wiki/Imperial_Regalia_of_Brazil "Imperial Regalia of Brazil") twice each year at the opening and closing of the General Assembly.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999133–134-81)[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_254–55-82) Pedro II held politicians and government officials to the strict standards which he exemplified.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESkidmore199948-83) The Emperor adopted a strict policy for the selection of civil servants based on morality and merit.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999163-84)[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho200783-85) To set the standard, he lived simply, once having said: "I also understand that useless expenditure is the same as stealing from the Nation".[[85]](#cite_note-86) [Balls](/wiki/Ballroom_dance "Ballroom dance") and assemblies of the Court ceased after 1852.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho200780-79)[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_253-87) He also refused to request or allow his [civil list](/wiki/Civil_list "Civil list") amount of R$800,000 per year (US$405,000 or £90,000 in 1840) to be raised from the declaration of his majority until his dethronement almost fifty years later.[[87]](#cite_note-88)

### Patron of arts and sciences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Patron of arts and sciences")]

[![Photograph of a man with a full beard and dressed in a dark frock coat who is seated at a table holding a book with bookshelves in the background](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Pedro_II_1858.jpg/250px-Pedro_II_1858.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pedro_II_1858.jpg)

Pedro II around age 32, c.1858. In the 1850s, books begin to feature prominently in his portraits, a reference to his role as advocate for education.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz1998326-89)

"I was born to devote myself to culture and sciences," the Emperor remarked in his private journal during 1862.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_2104-90)[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho200777-91) Subjects which interested Pedro II were wide-ranging, including [anthropology](/wiki/Anthropology "Anthropology"), [history](/wiki/History "History"), [geography](/wiki/Geography "Geography"), [geology](/wiki/Geology "Geology"), [medicine](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine"), [law](/wiki/Law "Law"), [religious studies](/wiki/Religious_studies "Religious studies"), [philosophy](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy"), [painting](/wiki/Painting "Painting"), [sculpture](/wiki/Sculpture "Sculpture"), [theater](/wiki/Theater "Theater"), [music](/wiki/Music "Music"), [chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry"), [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics"), [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy"), [poetry](/wiki/Poetry "Poetry"), and [technology](/wiki/Technology "Technology") among others.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_299-92)[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999542-93) By the end of his reign, there were three libraries in São Cristóvão palace containing more than 60,000 books.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007227-94) A passion for [linguistics](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics") prompted him throughout his life to study new languages, and he was able to speak and write not only Portuguese but also [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), French, German, English, Italian, Spanish, Greek, Arabic, [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew "Hebrew"), [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit"), Chinese, [Occitan](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language"), and [Tupi](/wiki/Tupi_language "Tupi language").[[94]](#cite_note-95) He became the first Brazilian photographer when he acquired a [daguerreotype](/wiki/Daguerreotype "Daguerreotype") camera in March 1840.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVasquez200377-96)[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz1998345-97) He set up one laboratory in São Cristóvão devoted to photography and another to chemistry and physics. He also had an astronomical observatory constructed.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999117-98)

The Emperor considered education to be of national importance and was himself a concrete example of the value of learning.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999118–119-99) He remarked: "Were I not an Emperor, I would like to be a teacher. I do not know of a task more noble than to direct young minds and prepare the men of tomorrow."[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_294–95-100) His reign saw the creation of the [Brazilian Historic and Geographic Institute](/wiki/Brazilian_Historic_and_Geographic_Institute "Brazilian Historic and Geographic Institute") to promote research and preservation in the historical, geographical, cultural, and social sciences.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz1998126-101) The Imperial Academy of Music and National Opera[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz1998152-102) and the [Pedro II School](/wiki/Col%C3%A9gio_Pedro_II_(Rio_de_Janeiro) "Colégio Pedro II (Rio de Janeiro)") were also founded, the latter serving as a model for schools throughout Brazil.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz1998150–151-103) The [Imperial Academy of the Fine Arts](/wiki/Escola_Nacional_de_Belas_Artes "Escola Nacional de Belas Artes"), established by his father, received further strengthening and support.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz1998144-104) Using his civil list income, Pedro II provided scholarships for Brazilian students to study at universities, art schools, and conservatories of music in Europe.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999119-105)[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho200799-106) He also financed the creation of the [Institute Pasteur](/wiki/Institute_Pasteur "Institute Pasteur"), helped underwrite the construction of Wagner's [Bayreuth Festspielhaus](/wiki/Bayreuth_Festspielhaus "Bayreuth Festspielhaus"), as well as subscribing to similar projects.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007226–228-107) His efforts were recognized both at home and abroad. [Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin") said of him: "The Emperor does so much for science, that every scientific man is bound to show him the utmost respect".[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002200-108)[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_2182-109)

Pedro II became a member of the [Royal Society](/wiki/Royal_Society "Royal Society"), the [Russian Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Russian_Academy_of_Sciences "Russian Academy of Sciences"), [The Royal Academies for Science and the Arts of Belgium](/wiki/The_Royal_Academies_for_Science_and_the_Arts_of_Belgium "The Royal Academies for Science and the Arts of Belgium") and the [American Geographical Society](/wiki/American_Geographical_Society "American Geographical Society").[[109]](#cite_note-110) In 1875, he was elected to the [French Academy of Sciences](/wiki/French_Academy_of_Sciences "French Academy of Sciences"), an honor previously granted to only two other heads of state: [Peter the Great](/wiki/Peter_the_Great "Peter the Great") and [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte").[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007172-111)[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_2255-112) He exchanged letters with scientists, philosophers, musicians and other intellectuals. Many of his correspondents became his friends, including [Richard Wagner](/wiki/Richard_Wagner "Richard Wagner"), [Louis Pasteur](/wiki/Louis_Pasteur "Louis Pasteur"), [Louis Agassiz](/wiki/Louis_Agassiz "Louis Agassiz"), [John Greenleaf Whittier](/wiki/John_Greenleaf_Whittier "John Greenleaf Whittier"), [Michel Eugène Chevreul](/wiki/Michel_Eug%C3%A8ne_Chevreul "Michel Eugène Chevreul"), [Alexander Graham Bell](/wiki/Alexander_Graham_Bell "Alexander Graham Bell"), [Henry Wadsworth Longfellow](/wiki/Henry_Wadsworth_Longfellow "Henry Wadsworth Longfellow"), [Arthur de Gobineau](/wiki/Arthur_de_Gobineau "Arthur de Gobineau"), [Frédéric Mistral](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Mistral "Frédéric Mistral"), [Alessandro Manzoni](/wiki/Alessandro_Manzoni "Alessandro Manzoni"), Alexandre Herculano, [Camilo Castelo Branco](/wiki/Camilo_Castelo_Branco "Camilo Castelo Branco"), and [James Cooley Fletcher](/wiki/James_Cooley_Fletcher "James Cooley Fletcher").[[112]](#cite_note-113) His erudition amazed [Friedrich Nietzsche](/wiki/Friedrich_Nietzsche "Friedrich Nietzsche") when the two met.[[113]](#cite_note-114) [Victor Hugo](/wiki/Victor_Hugo "Victor Hugo") told the Emperor: "Sire, you are a great citizen, you are the grandson of [Marcus Aurelius](/wiki/Marcus_Aurelius "Marcus Aurelius")," and [Alexandre Herculano](/wiki/Alexandre_Herculano "Alexandre Herculano") called him a "Prince whom the general opinion holds as the foremost of his era because of his gifted mind, and due to the constant application of that gift to the sciences and culture."[[114]](#cite_note-115)

### Clash with the British Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Clash with the British Empire")]

See also: [Christie Question](/wiki/Christie_Question "Christie Question")

[![Photograph showing a group of men and women standing at the foot of stairs leading up to a white house with dark shutters](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Familia_imperial_1861.jpg/500px-Familia_imperial_1861.jpg)](/wiki/File:Familia_imperial_1861.jpg)

Pedro II at age 35 along with his wife and daughters visiting a farm in southern [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais") province, 1861

At the end of 1859, Pedro II departed on a trip to provinces north of the capital, visiting [Espírito Santo](/wiki/Esp%C3%ADrito_Santo "Espírito Santo"), [Bahia](/wiki/Bahia "Bahia"), [Sergipe](/wiki/Sergipe "Sergipe"), [Alagoas](/wiki/Alagoas "Alagoas"), [Pernambuco](/wiki/Pernambuco "Pernambuco"), and [Paraíba](/wiki/Para%C3%ADba "Paraíba"). He returned in February 1860 after four months. The trip was a huge success, with the Emperor welcomed everywhere with warmth and joy.[[115]](#cite_note-116) The first half of the 1860s saw peace and prosperity in Brazil. [Civil liberties](/wiki/Civil_liberties "Civil liberties") were maintained.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_1200-117)[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999192-118) [Freedom of speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_speech "Freedom of speech") had existed since Brazil's independence and was strongly defended by Pedro II.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho200784-119)[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBesouchet1993508-120) He found newspapers from the capital and from the provinces an ideal way to keep track of public opinion and the nation's overall situation.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho200779-121) Another means of monitoring the Empire was through direct contacts with his subjects. One opportunity for this was during regular Tuesday and Saturday public audiences, where anyone of any social class, including slaves, could gain admittance and present their petitions and stories.[[121]](#cite_note-122) Visits to schools, colleges, prisons, exhibitions, factories, barracks, and other public appearances presented further opportunities to gather first-hand information.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999184-123)

This tranquility temporarily disappeared when the British consul in Rio de Janeiro, [William Dougal Christie](/wiki/William_Dougal_Christie "William Dougal Christie"), nearly sparked a war between his nation and Brazil. Christie sent an [ultimatum](/wiki/Ultimatum "Ultimatum") containing bullying demands arising out of two minor incidents at the end of 1861 and beginning of 1862. The first was the sinking of a British merchant [barque](/wiki/Barque "Barque") on the coast of Rio Grande do Sul after which its goods were pillaged by local inhabitants. The second was the arrest of a group of drunken British sailors who were causing a disturbance in the streets of Rio.[[123]](#cite_note-124)

The Brazilian government refused to yield, and Christie [issued orders for British warships](/wiki/Gunboat_diplomacy "Gunboat diplomacy") to capture Brazilian merchant vessels as indemnity.[[124]](#cite_note-125) Brazil prepared for what was seen as an imminent conflict. Pedro II was the main reason for Brazil's resistance; he rejected any suggestion of yielding.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999191-126)[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_1209-127) This response came as a surprise to Christie, who changed his tenor and proposed a peaceful settlement through international arbitration.[[127]](#cite_note-128) The Brazilian government presented its demands and, upon seeing the British government's position weaken, severed diplomatic ties with Britain in June 1863.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007105-129)[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_1211-130)

## Paraguayan War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Paraguayan War")]

Main article: [Pedro II of Brazil in the Paraguayan War](/wiki/Pedro_II_of_Brazil_in_the_Paraguayan_War "Pedro II of Brazil in the Paraguayan War")

See also: [Uruguayan War](/wiki/Uruguayan_War "Uruguayan War") and [Paraguayan War](/wiki/Paraguayan_War "Paraguayan War")

### First Fatherland Volunteer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: First Fatherland Volunteer")]

Main article: [Fatherland Volunteers](/wiki/Fatherland_Volunteers "Fatherland Volunteers")

[![Photograph of a seated, bearded man dressed in a dark suit and vest](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Pedro_II_1865_01.jpg/250px-Pedro_II_1865_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pedro_II_1865_01.jpg)

Pedro II at age 39, 1865

As war with the British Empire threatened, Brazil had to turn its attention to its southern frontiers. Another civil war had begun in [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay") as its political parties turned against each other.[[130]](#cite_note-131) The internal conflict led to the murder of Brazilians and looting of their property in Uruguay.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_1220-132) Brazil's government decided to intervene, fearful of giving any impression of weakness in the face of conflict with the British.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007107-133) A Brazilian army invaded Uruguay in December 1864, beginning the brief [Uruguayan War](/wiki/Uruguayan_War "Uruguayan War"), which ended in February 1865.[[133]](#cite_note-134) Meanwhile, the president of Paraguay, [Francisco Solano López](/wiki/Francisco_Solano_L%C3%B3pez "Francisco Solano López"), took advantage of the situation to establish his country as a regional power. The [Paraguayan Army](/wiki/Paraguayan_Army "Paraguayan Army") invaded the Brazilian province of [Mato Grosso](/wiki/Mato_Grosso "Mato Grosso") (the area known after 1977 as the state of [Mato Grosso do Sul](/wiki/Mato_Grosso_do_Sul "Mato Grosso do Sul")), triggering the [Paraguayan War](/wiki/Paraguayan_War "Paraguayan War"). Four months later, Paraguayan troops invaded [Argentine](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") territory as a prelude to an attack on [Rio Grande do Sul](/wiki/Rio_Grande_do_Sul "Rio Grande do Sul").[[134]](#cite_note-135)

Aware of the anarchy in Rio Grande do Sul and the incapacity and incompetence of its military chiefs to resist the Paraguayan army, Pedro II decided to go to the front in person.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_1228-136) Upon receiving objections from the cabinet, the General Assembly and the [Council of State](/wiki/Privy_council "Privy council"), Pedro II pronounced: "If they can prevent me from going as an Emperor, they cannot prevent me from abdicating and going as a Fatherland Volunteer"—an allusion to those Brazilians who volunteered to go to war and became known throughout the nation as the "Fatherland Volunteers".[[136]](#cite_note-137) The monarch himself was popularly called the "number-one volunteer".[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVainfas2002200-108)[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz1998300-138) Given permission to leave, Pedro II disembarked in Rio Grande do Sul in July and proceeded from there by land.[[138]](#cite_note-139) He travelled overland by horse and wagon, sleeping at night in a campaign tent.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007112-140) In September, Pedro II arrived in [Uruguaiana](/wiki/Uruguaiana "Uruguaiana"), a Brazilian town occupied by a besieged Paraguayan army.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007114-141)[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon1975745-142)

The Emperor rode within rifle-shot of Uruguaiana, but the Paraguayans did not attack him.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon1975744-143) To avoid further bloodshed, he offered terms of surrender to the Paraguayan commander, who accepted.[[143]](#cite_note-144) Pedro II's coordination of the military operations and his personal example played a decisive role in successfully repulsing the Paraguayan invasion of Brazilian territory.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999205-145) Before returning to Rio de Janeiro, he received the British diplomatic envoy [Edward Thornton](/wiki/Edward_Thornton_(diplomat) "Edward Thornton (diplomat)"), who apologized on behalf of [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") and the British Government for the crisis between the empires.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon1975748-146)[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_1237-147) The Emperor regarded this diplomatic victory over the most powerful nation of the world as sufficient and renewed friendly relations.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_1237-147)

### Total victory and its heavy costs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Total victory and its heavy costs")]

[![Photograph of a bearded man seated casually with crossed legs and wearing a military tunic with fringed epaulettes](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Pedro_II_Admiral_Brazil_1870.jpg/250px-Pedro_II_Admiral_Brazil_1870.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pedro_II_Admiral_Brazil_1870.jpg)

Dressed in an admiral's uniform at age 44, 1870—the war years had prematurely aged the Emperor[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007124-148)[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999247-149)

Against all expectations, the war continued for five years. During this period, Pedro II's time and energy were devoted to the war effort.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999193-150)[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007110-151) He tirelessly worked to raise and equip troops to reinforce the front lines and to push forward the fitting of new warships for the navy.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999202-152) The rape of women, widespread violence against civilians, ransacking and destruction of properties that had occurred during Paraguay's invasion of Brazilian territory had made a deep impression on him.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999206-153) He warned the Countess of Barral in November 1866 that "the war should be concluded as honor demands, cost what it cost."[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999230-154) "Difficulties, setbacks, and [war-weariness](/wiki/War-weariness "War-weariness") had no effect on his quiet resolve", said Barman. Mounting casualties did not distract him from advancing what he saw as Brazil's righteous cause, and he stood prepared to personally sacrifice his own throne to gain an honorable outcome.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999230-154) Writing in his journal a few years previously Pedro II remarked: "What sort of fear could I have? That they take the government from me? Many better kings than I have lost it, and to me it is no more than the weight of a cross which it is my duty to carry."[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999169-155)

At the same time, Pedro II worked to prevent quarrels between the national political parties from impairing the military response.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999219–224-156)[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007116–118-157) The Emperor prevailed over a serious political crisis in July 1868 resulting from a quarrel between the cabinet and [Luís Alves de Lima e Silva](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Alves_de_Lima_e_Silva "Luís Alves de Lima e Silva") (then-Marques and later Duke of Caxias), the commander-in-chief of the Brazilian forces in Paraguay. Caxias was also a politician and was a member of the opposing party to the ministry. The Emperor sided with him, leading to the cabinet's resignation. As Pedro II maneuvered to bring about a victorious outcome in the conflict with Paraguay, he threw his support behind the political parties and factions that seemed to be most useful in the effort. The reputation of the monarchy was harmed and its trusted position as an impartial mediator was severely impacted in the long term. He was unconcerned for his personal position, and regardless of the impact upon the imperial system, he determined to put the national interest ahead of any potential harm caused by such expediencies.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999224–225-158)

His refusal to accept anything short of total victory was pivotal in the outcome.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999206-153)[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007114–115-159) His tenacity was repaid with the news that López had died in battle on 1 March 1870, bringing the war to a close.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999229–230-160)[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007121-161) Pedro II turned down the General Assembly's suggestion to erect an [equestrian statue](/wiki/Equestrian_statue "Equestrian statue") of him to commemorate the victory and chose instead to use the money to build elementary schools.[[161]](#cite_note-162)

## Apogee

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Apogee")]

Main article: [Apogee of Pedro II of Brazil](/wiki/Apogee_of_Pedro_II_of_Brazil "Apogee of Pedro II of Brazil")

### Abolitionism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Abolitionism")]

[![Full length painted portrait of a bearded man wearing a gold crown, mantle and sword and grasping a long scepter](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Pedro_Am%C3%A9rico_-_D._Pedro_II_na_abertura_da_Assembl%C3%A9ia_Geral.jpg/250px-Pedro_Am%C3%A9rico_-_D._Pedro_II_na_abertura_da_Assembl%C3%A9ia_Geral.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pedro_Am%C3%A9rico_-_D._Pedro_II_na_abertura_da_Assembl%C3%A9ia_Geral.jpg)

Pedro II at age 46 delivering the [speech from the throne](/wiki/Speech_from_the_throne "Speech from the throne") wearing the [Imperial Regalia](/wiki/Imperial_Regalia_of_Brazil "Imperial Regalia of Brazil"), 1872

In the 1870s, progress was made in both social and political spheres as segments of society benefited from the reforms and shared in the increasing prosperity.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlivieri199937-163) Brazil's international reputation for political stability and investment potential greatly improved. The Empire was seen as a modern and progressive nation unequaled, with the exception of the United States, in the Americas.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_29-164) The economy began growing rapidly and immigration flourished. Railroad, shipping and other modernization projects were adopted. With "slavery destined for extinction and other reforms projected, the prospects for 'moral and material advances' seemed vast."[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999240-165)

In 1870, few Brazilians opposed slavery and even fewer openly condemned it. Pedro II, who did not own slaves, was one of the few who did oppose slavery.[[165]](#cite_note-166) Its abolition was a delicate subject. Slaves were used by all classes, from the richest to the poorest.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlivieri199943-167)[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007130-168) Pedro II wanted to end the practice gradually to soften the impact to the national economy.[[168]](#cite_note-169) With no constitutional authority to directly intervene to abolish slavery, the Emperor would need to use all his skills to convince, influence, and gather support among politicians to achieve his goal.[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999210-170)[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007132–136-171) His first open move occurred back in 1850, when he threatened to abdicate unless the General Assembly declared the [Atlantic slave trade](/wiki/Atlantic_slave_trade "Atlantic slave trade") illegal.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlivieri199944-172)[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_1166-173)

Having dealt with the overseas supply of new slaves, Pedro II turned his attention in the early 1860s to removing the remaining source: enslavement of children born to slaves.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007132-174)[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_2162-175) Legislation was drafted at his initiative, but the conflict with Paraguay delayed discussion of the proposal in the General Assembly.[[175]](#cite_note-176) Pedro II openly asked for the gradual eradication of slavery in the [speech from the throne](/wiki/Speech_from_the_throne "Speech from the throne") of 1867.[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007134-177) He was heavily criticized, and his move was condemned as "national suicide."[[177]](#cite_note-178) Critics argued "that abolition was his personal desire and not that of the nation."[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007136-179) He consciously ignored the growing political damage to his image and to the monarchy in consequence of his support for abolition.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999224–225-158) Eventually, a bill pushed through by Prime Minister [José Paranhos](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Paranhos "José Paranhos"), was enacted as the [Law of Free Birth](/wiki/Law_of_Free_Birth "Law of Free Birth") on 28 September 1871, under which all children born to slave women after that date were considered free born.[[179]](#cite_note-180)

### To Europe and North Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: To Europe and North Africa")]

[![A large group of men and women are gathered below the head of the Sphinx with the Great Pyramid looming behind](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Pedro_II_of_Brazil_in_Egypt_1871.jpg/500px-Pedro_II_of_Brazil_in_Egypt_1871.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pedro_II_of_Brazil_in_Egypt_1871.jpg)

French archaeologist [Auguste Mariette](/wiki/Auguste_Mariette "Auguste Mariette") (seated, far left) and Pedro II (seated, far right) during the Emperor's visit to the [Giza Necropolis](/wiki/Giza_Necropolis "Giza Necropolis") at the end of 1871

On 25 May 1871, Pedro II and his wife traveled to Europe.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999236-181)[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_2175-182) He had long desired to vacation abroad. When news arrived that his younger daughter, the 23-year-old Leopoldina, had died in Vienna of [typhoid fever](/wiki/Typhoid_fever "Typhoid fever") on 7 February, he finally had a pressing reason to venture outside the Empire.[[182]](#cite_note-183) Upon arriving in [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon"), Portugal, he immediately went to the [*Janelas Verdes* palace](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Ancient_Art "National Museum of Ancient Art"), where he met with his stepmother, Amélie of Leuchtenberg. The two had not seen each other in forty years, and the meeting was emotional. Pedro II remarked in his journal: "I cried from happiness and also from sorrow seeing my Mother so affectionate toward me but so aged and so sick."[[183]](#cite_note-184)

The Emperor proceeded to visit Spain, Great Britain, Belgium, Germany, Austria, Italy, Egypt, Greece, Switzerland, and France. In [Coburg](/wiki/Coburg "Coburg"), he visited his daughter's tomb.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007147-185)[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999237–238-186) He found this to be "a time of release and freedom". He traveled under the assumed name "Dom Pedro de Alcântara", insisting upon being treated informally and staying only in hotels.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999236-181)[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007146–147-187) He spent his days sightseeing and conversing with scientists and other intellectuals with whom he shared interests.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999236-181)[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007147-185) The European sojourn proved to be a success, and his demeanor and curiosity won respectful notices in the nations which he visited. The prestige of both Brazil and Pedro II were further enhanced during the tour when news came from Brazil that the Law of Free Birth, abolishing the last source of enslavement, had been ratified. The imperial party returned to Brazil in triumph on 31 March 1872.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999240-165)

### Religious Issue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Religious Issue")]

Main article: [Religious Issue](/wiki/Religious_Issue "Religious Issue")

[![Half-length photographic portrait of an older man with white hair and beard dressed in a dark jacket and necktie](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Pedro_II_circa_1887b_transparent.png/250px-Pedro_II_circa_1887b_transparent.png)](/wiki/File:Pedro_II_circa_1887b_transparent.png)

Pedro, c. 1887

Soon after returning to Brazil, Pedro II was faced with an unexpected crisis. The Brazilian clergy had long been understaffed, undisciplined and poorly educated, leading to a great loss of respect for the Catholic Church.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999254-188)[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007151-189) The imperial government had embarked upon a program of reform to address these deficiencies.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999254-188) As Catholicism was the state religion, the government exercised a great deal of control over Church affairs, paying clerical salaries, appointing parish priests, nominating bishops, ratifying [papal bulls](/wiki/Papal_bull "Papal bull") and overseeing seminaries.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999254-188)[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007150-190) In pursuing reform, the government selected bishops who satisfied its criteria for education, support for reform and moral fitness.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999254-188)[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007151-189) However, as more capable men began to fill the clerical ranks, resentment of government control over the Church increased.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999254-188)[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007151-189)

The bishops of [Olinda](/wiki/Olinda "Olinda") and [Belém](/wiki/Bel%C3%A9m "Belém") (in the provinces of Pernambuco and [Pará](/wiki/Par%C3%A1 "Pará"), respectively) were two of the new generation of educated and zealous Brazilian clerics. They had been influenced by the [ultramontanism](/wiki/Ultramontanism "Ultramontanism"), which spread among Catholics in this period. In 1872, they ordered [Freemasons](/wiki/Freemasons "Freemasons") expelled from [lay brotherhoods](/wiki/Lay_brotherhoods "Lay brotherhoods").[[190]](#cite_note-191) While European Freemasonry often tended towards [anti-clericalism](/wiki/Anti-clericalism "Anti-clericalism"), things were much different in Brazil where membership in Masonic orders was common—although Pedro II himself was not a Freemason.[[191]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999255–256-192) The government headed by the Viscount of Rio Branco tried on two separate occasions to persuade the bishops to repeal, but they refused. This led to their trial and conviction by the [Superior Court of Justice](/wiki/Superior_Court_of_Justice_(Brazil) "Superior Court of Justice (Brazil)"). In 1874, they were sentenced four years at hard labor, although the Emperor commuted this to imprisonment only.[[192]](#cite_note-193)

Pedro II played a decisive role by unequivocally backing the government's actions.[[193]](#cite_note-194) He was a conscientious adherent of Catholicism, which he viewed as advancing important civilizing and civic values. While he avoided anything that could be considered unorthodox, he felt free to think and behave independently.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999253-195) The Emperor accepted new ideas, such as [Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin")'s theory of [evolution](/wiki/Evolution "Evolution"), of which he remarked that "the laws that he [Darwin] has discovered glorify the Creator".[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBesouchet199334-196) He was moderate in his religious beliefs but could not accept disrespect to civil law and government authority.[[196]](#cite_note-197) As he told his son-in-law: "[The government] has to ensure that the constitution is obeyed. In these proceedings there is no desire to protect masonry; but rather the goal of upholding the rights of the civilian power."[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999257-198) The crisis was resolved in September 1875 after the Emperor grudgingly agreed to grant full amnesty to the bishops and the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") annulled the interdicts.[[198]](#cite_note-199)

### To the United States, Europe, and Middle East

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: To the United States, Europe, and Middle East")]

[![Photograph of three people, two women and a bearded man, seated on a park bench with six other men standing in back and waterfalls in the distance](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Pedro_II_of_Brazil_Niagara_Falls_1876.png/500px-Pedro_II_of_Brazil_Niagara_Falls_1876.png)](/wiki/File:Pedro_II_of_Brazil_Niagara_Falls_1876.png)

Pedro II (seated, right) at [Niagara Falls](/wiki/Niagara_Falls "Niagara Falls"), 1876

Once again, the Emperor traveled abroad, this time going to the United States for the Centennial Exposition of the Declaration of Independence. He was accompanied by his faithful servant Rafael, who had raised him from childhood.[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBesouchet1993275-200) Pedro II arrived in New York City on 15 April 1876, and set out from there to travel throughout the country; going as far as [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco") in the west, [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") in the south, [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), and north to [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"), Ontario, Canada.[[200]](#cite_note-201) The trip was "an unalloyed triumph", Pedro II making a deep impression on the American people with his simplicity and kindness and making him a favorite with the US press who dubbed him "the Yankee Emperor."[[201]](#cite_note-202) He then crossed the Atlantic, where he visited Denmark, Sweden, Finland, Russia, the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), Greece, the [Holy Land](/wiki/Holy_Land#Christianity "Holy Land"), Egypt, Italy, Austria, Germany, France, Britain, Ireland,[[202]](#cite_note-DPII-203) the Netherlands, Switzerland, and Portugal.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999280–282-204)[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_2222-205) He returned to Brazil on 22 September 1877.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999286-206)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Imatrankosken_alapaviljonki_2017_05.jpg/250px-Imatrankosken_alapaviljonki_2017_05.jpg)](/wiki/File:Imatrankosken_alapaviljonki_2017_05.jpg)

Engraved signature by Pedro II while visiting the [Imatrankoski](/wiki/Imatrankoski "Imatrankoski") rapids in [Imatra](/wiki/Imatra "Imatra"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"); 1876.[[206]](#cite_note-207)

Pedro II's trips abroad made a deep psychological impact. While traveling, he was largely freed of the restrictions imposed by his office.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999245-208) Under the pseudonym "Pedro de Alcântara", he enjoyed moving about as an ordinary person, even taking a train journey solely with his wife. Only while touring abroad could the Emperor shake off the formal existence and demands of the life he knew in Brazil.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999245-208) It became more difficult to reacclimate to his routine as head of state upon returning.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBesouchet1993248-209) Upon his sons' early deaths, the Emperor's faith in the monarchy's future had evaporated. His trips abroad now made him resentful of the emperorship assigned to him at the age of five. If he previously had no interest in securing the throne for the next generation, he now had no desire to keep it going during his own lifetime.[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBesouchet1993248,_253-210)

## Decline and fall

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Decline and fall")]

Main article: [Decline and fall of Pedro II of Brazil](/wiki/Decline_and_fall_of_Pedro_II_of_Brazil "Decline and fall of Pedro II of Brazil")

### Decline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Decline")]

[![Photographic portrait of a man with a white beard seated in an armchair and holding a small book in his left hand while supporting his head with his right hand](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Pedro_II_of_Brazil_Paris_1887.jpg/250px-Pedro_II_of_Brazil_Paris_1887.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pedro_II_of_Brazil_Paris_1887.jpg)

Pedro II at age 61, in 1887. He became tired of the monarchy and resigned to its demise

During the 1880s, Brazil continued to prosper, and social diversity increased markedly, including the first organized push for [women's rights](/wiki/Women%27s_rights "Women's rights").[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999319-211) On the other hand, letters written by Pedro II reveal a man grown world-weary with age and having an increasingly alienated and pessimistic outlook.[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999298–299-212) He remained respectful of his duty and was meticulous in performing the tasks demanded of the imperial office, albeit often without enthusiasm.[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999299-213) Because of his increasing "indifference towards the fate of the regime" and his lack of action in support of the imperial system once it was challenged, historians have attributed the "prime, perhaps sole, responsibility" for the dissolution of the monarchy to the Emperor himself.[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_3126-214)[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999399-215)

After their experience of the perils and obstacles of government, the political figures who had arisen during the 1830s saw the Emperor as providing a fundamental source of authority essential for governing and for national survival.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999317-32) These elder statesmen began to die off or retire from government until, by the 1880s, they had almost entirely been replaced by a newer generation of politicians who had no experience of the early years of Pedro II's reign. They had only known a stable administration and prosperity and saw no reason to uphold and defend the imperial office as a unifying force beneficial to the nation.[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999318-216) To them, Pedro II was merely an old and increasingly sick man who had steadily eroded his position by taking an active role in politics for decades. Before he had been above criticism, but now his every action and inaction prompted meticulous scrutiny and open criticism. Many young politicians had become apathetic toward the monarchic regime and, when the time came, they would do nothing to defend it.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999258–259,_317–318,_349-217) Pedro II's achievements went unremembered and unconsidered by the ruling elites. By his very success, the Emperor had made his position seem unnecessary.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999xiv-218)

The lack of an heir who could feasibly provide a new direction for the nation also diminished the long-term prospects of the Brazilian monarchy. The Emperor loved his daughter Isabel, but he considered the idea of a female successor as antithetical to the role required of Brazil's ruler. He viewed the death of his two sons as being a sign that the Empire was destined to be supplanted.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999262-219) Resistance to accepting a female ruler was also shared by the political establishment.[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999268-220) Even though the Constitution allowed female succession to the throne, Brazil was still very traditional, and only a male successor was thought capable as head of state.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999130-74)

### Abolition of slavery and coup d'état

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Abolition of slavery and coup d'état")]

See also: [Proclamation of the Republic (Brazil)](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_Republic_(Brazil) "Proclamation of the Republic (Brazil)")

[![A group of people assembled on a columned porch at the top of a flight of steps, with one older woman seated, one younger woman leaning on the arm of an older bearded man, two younger men, and three small boys](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Fam%C3%ADlia_Imperial_por_Otto_Hees.jpg/330px-Fam%C3%ADlia_Imperial_por_Otto_Hees.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fam%C3%ADlia_Imperial_por_Otto_Hees.jpg)

The last picture of the imperial family in Brazil, 1889

By June 1887, the Emperor's health had considerably worsened, and his personal doctors suggested going to Europe for medical treatment.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_353–56-221) While in [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") he passed two weeks between life and death, even being [anointed](/wiki/Anointing_of_the_Sick_in_the_Catholic_Church "Anointing of the Sick in the Catholic Church").[[221]](#cite_note-222) While on a bed recovering, on 22 May 1888 he received news that [slavery had been abolished](/wiki/Lei_%C3%81urea "Lei Áurea") in Brazil.[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_362-223) With a weak voice and tears in his eyes, he said, "Great people! Great people!"[[223]](#cite_note-224) Pedro II returned to Brazil and disembarked in Rio de Janeiro in August 1888.[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007200-225)[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon19751438-226) The "whole country welcomed him with an enthusiasm never seen before. From the capital, from the provinces, from everywhere, arrived proofs of affection and veneration."[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_364-227) With the devotion expressed by Brazilians upon the return of the Emperor and the Empress from Europe, the monarchy seemed to enjoy unshakable support and to be at the height of its popularity.[[227]](#cite_note-228)

The nation enjoyed great international prestige during the final years of the Empire, and it had become an [emerging power](/wiki/Emerging_power "Emerging power") within the international arena.[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETopik200056-229)[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999306-230) Predictions of economic and labor disruption caused by the abolition of slavery failed to materialize and the 1888 coffee harvest was successful.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999346-231) The end of slavery had resulted in an explicit shift of support to [republicanism](/wiki/Republicanism "Republicanism") by rich and powerful coffee farmers who held great political, economic, and social power in the country.[[231]](#cite_note-232) Republicanism was an elitist creed which never flourished in Brazil, with little support in the provinces.[[232]](#cite_note-233) The combination of republican ideas and the dissemination of [positivism](/wiki/Positivism "Positivism") among the army's lower and medium officer ranks led to indiscipline among the corps and became a serious threat to the monarchy. They dreamed of a dictatorial republic, which they believed would be superior to the monarchy.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007195-234)[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999353-235)

Although there was no desire in Brazil among the majority of the population to change the [form of government](/wiki/Form_of_government "Form of government"), the civilian republicans began pressuring army officers to overthrow the monarchy.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErmakoff2006189-236)[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz1998450-237) They launched a [coup d'état](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_Republic_(Brazil) "Proclamation of the Republic (Brazil)"), arrested Prime Minister [Afonso Celso, Viscount of Ouro Preto](/wiki/Afonso_Celso,_Viscount_of_Ouro_Preto "Afonso Celso, Viscount of Ouro Preto") and instituted the republic on 15 November 1889.[[237]](#cite_note-238) The few people who witnessed what occurred did not realize that it was a rebellion.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwarcz1998459-239)[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELira_1977,_Vol_396-240) Historian Lídia Besouchet noted that "[r]arely has a revolution been so minor."[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBesouchet1993538-241) During the ordeal, Pedro II showed no emotion as if unconcerned about the outcome.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999361-242) He dismissed all suggestions for quelling the rebellion that politicians and military leaders put forward.[[242]](#cite_note-243) When he heard the news of his deposition he simply commented: "If it is so, it will be my retirement. I have worked too hard and I am tired. I will go rest then."[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007218-244) He and his family were sent into exile in Europe on 17 November.[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007220-245)

## Exile and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Exile and legacy")]

### Last years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_II_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Last years")]

Main article: [Exile and death of Pedro II of Brazil](/wiki/Exile_and_death_of_Pedro_II_of_Brazil "Exile and death of Pedro II of Brazil")

[![Photograph showing a white-bearded man, hands folded on stomach, dressed in a uniform with sash and chain of office and lying in state on cushions with a book under the cushion at the head](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Atelier_Nadar_-_Pedro_II._%281825-1891%29%2C_K%C3%B6nig_von_Brasilien%2C_auf_seinem_Totenbett_in_Paris_%28Zeno_Fotografie%29.jpg/330px-Atelier_Nadar_-_Pedro_II._%281825-1891%29%2C_K%C3%B6nig_von_Brasilien%2C_auf_seinem_Totenbett_in_Paris_%28Zeno_Fotografie%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Atelier_Nadar_-_Pedro_II._(1825-1891),_K%C3%B6nig_von_Brasilien,_auf_seinem_Totenbett_in_Paris_(Zeno_Fotografie).jpg)

Pedro, clad in court dress uniform, on his bier, 6 December 1891: the book beneath the pillow under his head symbolized that his mind rests upon knowledge even in death

Teresa Cristina died three weeks after their arrival in Europe, and Isabel and her family moved to another place while Pedro settled first in [Cannes](/wiki/Cannes "Cannes") and later in Paris.[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007234–235-246)[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999371,_377-247) Pedro's last couple of years were lonely and melancholic, as he lived in modest hotels without money and writing in his journal of dreams in which he was allowed to return to Brazil.[[247]](#cite_note-248) He never supported a restoration of the monarchy, once stating that he had no desire "to return to the position which I occupied, especially not by means of conspiracy of any sort."[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999380-249) One day he caught an infection that progressed quickly into [pneumonia](/wiki/Pneumonia "Pneumonia").[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007238-250)[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBesouchet199329-251) Pedro rapidly declined and died at 00:35 on 5 December 1891 surrounded by his family.[[251]](#cite_note-252) His last words were "May God grant me these last wishes—peace and prosperity for Brazil".[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBesouchet199330-253) While the body was being prepared, a sealed package in the room was found, and next to it a message written by the Emperor himself: "It is soil from my country, I wish it to be placed in my coffin in case I die away from my fatherland."[[253]](#cite_note-254)

Isabel wished to hold a discreet and private burial ceremony, but she eventually agreed to the French government's request for a [state funeral](/wiki/State_funeral "State funeral").[[254]](#cite_note-255) On 9 December, thousands of mourners attended the ceremony at [La Madeleine](/wiki/La_Madeleine,_Paris "La Madeleine, Paris"). Aside from Pedro's family, these included: [Francesco II](/wiki/Francis_II_of_the_Two_Sicilies "Francis II of the Two Sicilies"), former king of the Two Sicilies; [Isabel II](/wiki/Isabella_II_of_Spain "Isabella II of Spain"), former queen of Spain; [Philippe, comte de Paris](/wiki/Philippe,_comte_de_Paris "Philippe, comte de Paris"); and other members of European royalty.[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon19751898-256)[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBesouchet1993617-257) Also present were General [Joseph Brugère](/wiki/Joseph_Brug%C3%A8re "Joseph Brugère"), representing President [Sadi Carnot](/wiki/Marie_Fran%C3%A7ois_Sadi_Carnot "Marie François Sadi Carnot"); the presidents of the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_France "Senate of France") and the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_of_France "Chamber of Deputies of France") as well as their members; diplomats; and other representatives of the French government.[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBesouchet1993618-258)[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007239-259) Nearly all members of the [Institut de France](/wiki/Institut_de_France "Institut de France") were in attendance.[[258]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007239-259)[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon19751899-260) Other governments from the Americas and Europe sent representatives, as did the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), [Persia](/wiki/Qajar_Iran "Qajar Iran"), [China](/wiki/Qing_Dynasty "Qing Dynasty"), and [Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan").[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBesouchet1993618-258) Following the services, the coffin was taken in procession to the railway station to begin its trip to Portugal. Around 300,000 people lined the route under incessant rain and cold.[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon19751900-261)[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBesouchet1993614-262) The journey continued on to the [Church of São Vicente de Fora](/wiki/Monastery_of_S%C3%A3o_Vicente_de_Fora "Monastery of São Vicente de Fora") near [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon"), where the body of Pedro was interred in the [Royal Pantheon of the House of Braganza](/wiki/Royal_Pantheon_of_the_House_of_Braganza "Royal Pantheon of the House of Braganza") on 12 December.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho2007240-263)[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon19751900–1902-264)
